reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong, self-contained opening paragraph that explains what deploy does and why it matters — immediately AEO-extractable.

The FeatureComparisonTable for deployment modes is the highlight of the page: all three modes side-by-side with duration, rollback support, and what runs where. A first-time reader can make the right choice after 10 seconds.
Hot-swap section is opinionated and actionable: explicit bullet lists of what it can and cannot update, plus a well-placed Warning about state drift. This prevents the most common dev mistake.
The CloudFormation stack states table maps abstract AWS states to plain-English meanings and concrete next steps — genuinely useful for troubleshooting without requiring CloudFormation expertise.
FlowDiagram gives a clear visual sequence of the five deployment phases, grounded in the actual deploy command source (validate → package → upload → deploy → post-deploy).
FAQ section spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS service behavior, and choosing/comparison questions. Ten pairs, appropriately concise.
Troubleshooting section is actionable throughout — every scenario ends with a copyable command or a clear diagnostic step, not vague advice.
The Tip callout about defaults:configure is well-timed and will save most first-time users from typing --stage and --region on every command.
problems: Stage name constraints stated as 'lowercase alphanumeric with dashes, at least 2 characters, and at most 12 characters' — the provided curated docs source only confirms 'Max 12 characters'. The lowercase, alphanumeric, dash-only, and 2-character-minimum constraints are not grounded in the provided source files. Any of these that are wrong will mislead readers at a critical early step.

The --profile flag is listed with alias -p, but the curated docs source table uses --awsProfile (not --profile). This naming discrepancy is unresolved in the provided sources and could cause reader confusion.

The --disableDriftDetection flag appears in the key options table without grounding in either provided source file (deploy command source or curated docs). It may be real, but it cannot be verified from the inline sources.
The 'secret:create' command referenced in the Troubleshooting > Secrets prompt section is not confirmed by any provided source file. The exact command name should be verified before publication.

The 'Dev mode stacks and deployment' section near the bottom of the page covers a hard blocker (deploying to a dev stack throws an error — confirmed in source: stpErrors.e141) but is buried after FAQ-adjacent content. A first-time user who accidentally ran stacktape dev first will not find this warning when they need it.
optionalImprovements: Verify the full stage name constraint (lowercase, alphanumeric, dashes only, 2-char min, 12-char max) against the runtime validator in src/domain. If the 2-char minimum and character-set rules are confirmed, keep them. If not confirmed, narrow the wording to just what the source supports ('at most 12 characters').

Resolve --profile vs --awsProfile against the actual CLI arg definitions in src/commands/deploy/index.ts or the global args parser. Use whichever name the source declares and update the key options table accordingly.

Move the 'Dev mode stacks and deployment' section or add a short callout within 'Running a deployment' — something like: 'If you previously ran stacktape dev for this stage, use a different stage name; deploying on top of a dev stack is blocked.' This is a hard error for first-timers and should be visible before they hit it.

Consider adding a one-line callout in the CI/CD context (either in the 'Running a deployment' section or the relevant FAQ answer) naming the STACKTAPE_API_KEY environment variable. It's only mentioned in the FAQ and the source curated docs, but for a CI/CD user it's a critical prerequisite.

The post-deploy FlowDiagram step mentions 'send notifications' but the page never explains how to configure alert channels or where the notifications go. Either link to /observability/alert-channels directly in that step's description, or drop 'send notifications' from the diagram step to avoid raising an unanswered question.

The 'Rollback' link in the CloudFormation states table points to /deployment-and-lifecycle/rollbacks, but the Troubleshooting section on ROLLBACK_COMPLETE state doesn't mention rollback as an option — only delete + redeploy. Consider adding a one-liner noting that stuck UPDATE_FAILED stacks can also be recovered with stacktape rollback.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Accurate description of the five deployment phases mapped directly to the source code phases (BUILD_AND_PACKAGE, UPLOAD, DEPLOY, POST_DEPLOY), with sensible user-facing split of validate/prepare vs package.

Hot-swap fallback behavior is correctly sourced: the exact warning string 'Hot-swap not possible; running full CloudFormation deploy.' matches the tuiManager.warn call in the deploy source, and the condition (first deploy always runs full) is accurate.

CloudFormation stack states table is production-grade — lists all major states, explains what each allows, and cross-references rollback docs. This is exactly what engineers need when a deploy gets stuck.

The --disableAutoRollback debugging workflow (disable → inspect UPDATE_FAILED → fix → redeploy or rollback) is well-explained and correctly sourced from the isAutoRollbackEnabled check in performFullDeploy.

Deployment modes comparison table (Full / Hot-swap / CodeBuild) is concise and decision-useful — typical duration estimates and build location row are high-value differentiators that help the reader choose the right mode.
10 FAQ pairs mixing Stacktape-specific, AWS behavior, and comparison questions. Covers questions with real search intent (how long does deploy take, how do I deploy from CI/CD).
Dev mode vs full deploy distinction is accurately sourced from the stpErrors.e141 check in the deploy command source (throwing when trying to deploy to an existing dev stack).
problems: The rollback CLI command is linked as `/cli/rollback` and called `stacktape rollback`, but the project's git status shows `docs/docs/cli/cf-rollback.mdx` as an existing CLI reference file. If the correct command is `stacktape cf:rollback`, the page gives readers a broken command they will copy into CI pipelines.

The post-deploy phase description omits user-defined `afterDeploy` hooks entirely. The FlowDiagram step only lists 'Sync hosting buckets, invalidate CDN caches, save stack info, send notifications' — but afterDeploy hooks (database migrations, post-deploy scripts) are a primary reason production engineers deploy at all. This is a critical omission for the stated audience.

The 'Automatic rollback' section says 'For new stacks that fail on first creation, the stack and all its resources are deleted automatically.' This is misleading: the stack shell remains in ROLLBACK_COMPLETE state (correctly explained in Troubleshooting). The two sections contradict each other and will confuse readers debugging a stuck stack name.

The key options table lists short aliases (-hs, -aco, -dar, -nc, -ptf, -ll, -p, -ddd, -prj) that do not appear anywhere in the provided source files. If any alias is wrong, engineers copying it from CI pipelines will get argument-parsing errors with no useful feedback.

The curated source lists 'Lambda environment variables' as hot-swappable, but the draft's 'What hot-swap can update' section omits this. Engineers relying on the page to decide whether hot-swap covers their change will make a wrong call if env var changes are in scope.
mandatoryFixes: Verify the rollback CLI command name: the repo has `docs/docs/cli/cf-rollback.mdx`, which strongly implies the command is `stacktape cf:rollback` (not `stacktape rollback`). Update both the command reference in the Troubleshooting section and the link target from `/cli/rollback` to the correct path. If the command is genuinely `stacktape rollback`, add a note explaining its relationship to the `cf-rollback` doc file.

Add afterDeploy hooks coverage to the post-deploy section. The FlowDiagram step description and the 'Post-deploy' prose section must mention that user-defined `afterDeploy` hooks run in this phase. Include a brief example (database migrations being the canonical case) and a link to the hooks reference. This is not an optional edge case — it is the primary production use of post-deploy.
optionalImprovements: Fix the ROLLBACK_COMPLETE contradiction: change 'the stack and all its resources are deleted automatically' in the Automatic rollback section to 'all resources are removed but the stack shell remains in ROLLBACK_COMPLETE state, blocking the stage name until deleted.' This aligns with the Troubleshooting section and removes the inconsistency.

Audit the flag alias column in the key options table against the actual CLI argument parser definitions (not just curated docs). If aliases cannot be verified, either remove the Alias column or add a note pointing to the CLI reference page as the authoritative list.

Add Lambda environment variables to the 'What hot-swap can update' bullet list. The curated source includes this, and it is a common reason developers try hot-swap — omitting it causes engineers to incorrectly conclude hot-swap won't work for their change.

The `--awsProfile` flag appears in the curated source but the draft uses `--profile`. Verify the canonical flag name against the CLI definition; pick one form and use it consistently on the page and in the flags table.

Consider a one-liner in the 'Running a deployment' section noting that defaults (stage, region, profile) can be persisted with `stacktape defaults:configure` so the Tip callout is not the only way readers discover this workflow.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Answer-first paragraph is exactly 67 words, self-contained, and quotes directly citable facts about atomic operations and per-stage CloudFormation stacks.

All three deployment modes (full, hot-swap, CodeBuild) receive roughly equivalent depth: prose rationale, a runnable bash example, and explicit when-to-use guidance. The FeatureComparisonTable is a high-quality citation target.

Hot-swap section is unusually well-grounded: the fallback warning message is quoted verbatim from the source code (`tuiManager.warn`), and the what-can/cannot-update bullet lists match `performHotswapDeploy` semantics precisely.

Decision support for opt-in features is solid throughout: hot-swap gets an explicit Warning callout about state drift, drift detection gets a recommended default stance for prod vs dev, and CodeBuild gets a clear when-not-to-use signal (simple Lambda deployments).

No forbidden patterns: no YAML Stacktape config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type imports, no fenced TypeScript blocks with defineConfig, no PropertiesTable, no multiple ApiReference components.

issues.statement: CodeBuild deployment works by zipping your git-tracked project files, uploading the archive to S3, and starting a CodeBuild build that runs the full deployment inside AWS.
issues.reason: The phrase 'git-tracked project files' is misleading. The `gitCreateZipArchive` function runs `git add -A` before archiving, which stages ALL files in the working directory (including new untracked files), not just previously tracked files. The archive includes everything not in `.gitignore`, even uncommitted and untracked files. This matters because users might think they need to commit new files before running `codebuild:deploy`.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace 'git-tracked project files' with 'your project files (respecting .gitignore)' or 'all non-gitignored files in your project directory, including uncommitted changes'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

issues.statement: Not all Lambda functions in your stack are eligible for hot-swap — edge functions and some framework-internal functions (such as Next.js API route handlers) are excluded and require a full deploy for code changes.
issues.reason: The parenthetical '(such as Next.js API route handlers)' is unsupported by the source. The `allLambdasEligibleForHotswap` getter in `src/domain/config-manager/index.ts` explicitly INCLUDES Next.js Lambda functions of type 'function' via `this.allNextjsLambdaFunctions.filter(({ type }) => type === 'function')` and all SSR web Lambda functions via `this.allSsrWebLambdaFunctions`. API route handlers in the Stacktape/open-next architecture are regular Lambda functions (type 'function'), so they ARE eligible for hot-swap. Only edge-lambda-function types are filtered out. The broader claim about edge functions being excluded is correct.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: 'Not all Lambda functions in your stack are eligible for hot-swap — edge functions are excluded and require a full deploy for code changes.' Remove the parenthetical about Next.js API route handlers entirely, or replace it with '(such as Next.js middleware functions running at the edge)' if that's accurate.

The draft is useful and mostly follows the requested structure, but it overstates several deploy behaviors that are not supported by the provided deploy command source. The highest-impact issues are around CloudFormation atomicity/rollback, detailed hot-swap capabilities, CLI flags, and several specific lifecycle claims that the source does not substantiate.

issues.statement: “The `stacktape deploy` command packages your application code, generates a CloudFormation template from your configuration, and creates or updates all AWS resources as a single atomic operation. If any resource fails to provision, the entire stack rolls back to its previous working state automatically.”
issues.reason: The source supports packaging, template preparation, and CloudFormation deployment, but it does not support the broad claim that all resources are created or updated as a single atomic operation or that every failure always rolls the entire stack back to the previous working state. The deploy code explicitly has paths where auto-rollback can be disabled and cleanup behavior is conditional.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow this to: “For full deployments, Stacktape prepares artifacts, generates a CloudFormation template, uploads artifacts, and asks CloudFormation to create or update the stack. When CloudFormation auto-rollback is enabled, Stacktape cleans up artifacts for rolled-back deployments except stack-monitoring failures.”

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Strong, self-contained first paragraph (≈70 words) that accurately covers the deploy command's phases, stage-to-stack mapping, and auto-rollback in one extractable block.
FlowDiagram steps match the source code phases (validate, package, upload, deploy, post-deploy) precisely — no invented steps.
Feature comparison table for Full / Hot-swap / CodeBuild deployment modes is the highest-value section on the page — concise, citeable, and decision-supporting.

Hot-swap section includes all four required decision-support elements: when to enable (code iteration in dev), when the default is fine (production), cost/tradeoff signal (CloudFormation drift), and an opinionated Warning callout.
CloudFormation stack-state table with a 'What you can do' column is exceptionally practical and directly grounded in CloudFormation state names referenced in the deploy source.

FAQ is exactly 10 questions and covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (hot-swap vs full, CodeBuild timing), AWS use-case (CF pricing, drift), and choosing/comparison (Stacktape vs raw CloudFormation, CI/CD setup).
Troubleshooting subsections map to real failure modes visible in the deploy source (UPDATE_FAILED cleanup path, ROLLBACK_COMPLETE guard, secret prompts).
Source-grounded hot-swap logic: draft correctly states Stacktape warns and falls back to full deploy when hot-swap is not possible — confirmed by the `tuiManager.warn` call in the source.
problems: The draft consistently links rollback to `/cli/rollback` and uses the command `stacktape rollback`, but the git-status shows `docs/docs/cli/cf-rollback.mdx` — the CLI naming convention (matching `cf-module-update.mdx` → `stacktape cf:module:update`, `codebuild-deploy.mdx` → `stacktape codebuild:deploy`) strongly implies the correct command is `stacktape cf:rollback` and the correct link is `/cli/cf-rollback`. Using the wrong command name in docs teaches readers a command that will error.

The `--preserveTempFiles` behaviour note ('Files are saved to `.stacktape/<invocation-id>/`') is sourced only from the curated docs hint, not from the provided source code. If this path is wrong, readers debugging generated templates will look in the wrong place.

The 'Deploying from the Stacktape Console' section is thin — one sentence of substance and two link-outs. It names GitOps and preview environments but doesn't explain what either means in two lines, making it almost empty for an extracted snippet.

The CodeBuild deployment section lacks a cost or tradeoff signal. CodeBuild has its own hourly pricing; this affects when it is economical vs local deployment. The section says 'more compute resources available' but gives no tradeoff anchoring (e.g. startup overhead, extra cost for small stacks).
mandatoryFixes: Verify and correct the rollback command and link. The file `docs/docs/cli/cf-rollback.mdx` in the git status strongly suggests the correct CLI reference path is `/cli/cf-rollback` and the command is `stacktape cf:rollback`. Replace every instance of `stacktape rollback` and `/cli/rollback` in the draft after confirming against the CLI source (`src/commands/cf-rollback/index.ts` or equivalent).
optionalImprovements: Add a brief cost/tradeoff note to the CodeBuild deployment section — at minimum one sentence noting that CodeBuild jobs incur separate AWS charges (per-minute compute pricing), so for fast Lambda-only stacks the overhead rarely pays off.

Expand 'Deploying from the Stacktape Console' to two or three sentences that characterise what Console-triggered deployments look like (e.g. push-to-deploy, automatic PR preview teardown) before linking out, so the section is a useful standalone snippet.

The 'Running a deployment' section's Tip callout references `defaults:configure` but the rest of the page uses `stacktape deploy` with explicit flags. A single line noting that once defaults are set users can omit `--stage` and `--region` would make the Tip more actionable in context.

issues.statement: For container workloads, hot-swap updates the task definition — including container images and environment variables — and the service configuration.
issues.reason: While technically accurate (compareEcsTaskDefinitions does check environment variables within ContainerDefinitions), the draft's phrasing implies that changing environment variables in your Stacktape config will be hot-swapped. In practice, most env-var changes come from config edits which alter the CloudFormation template at the TaskDefinition level. Hot-swap for containers is only eligible when the ONLY CF template property that changed is `ContainerDefinitions` (analyzeTaskDefinitionChange, line 26). Env-var changes that map to ContainerDefinitions ARE hot-swappable, but changes to other task-definition properties (memory, CPU, volumes, etc.) are not. The draft should clarify this nuance — 'environment variables' being hot-swappable for containers is conditional on the CF diff being scoped to ContainerDefinitions only.
issues.suggestedFix: Rephrase to: 'For container workloads, hot-swap updates the container definitions — including images, environment variables, health checks, secrets, and port mappings — but only when no other task-definition properties (memory, CPU, volumes) have changed.'

evidence.quote: const isHotswappable = updatedProperties.length === 1 && updatedProperties[0] === 'ContainerDefinitions';

issues.statement: The FeatureComparisonTable shows 'Typical duration' values of '1-10 min' for full deploy, 'Seconds' for hot-swap, and '3-15 min' for CodeBuild deploy.
issues.reason: These duration ranges are not grounded in any provided source file. While the curated docs mention '~5 seconds' for hot-swap and '47s' for a simple example deploy, neither source supports the specific ranges '1-10 min' for full deploy or '3-15 min' for CodeBuild deploy. These appear to be invented estimates. The FAQ answer later states hot-swap completes 'in seconds' (fine) and that stacks with databases take '3-10 minutes' (also ungrounded). These specific numbers could mislead readers into incorrect expectations.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove specific time ranges from the comparison table or soften to qualitative terms: 'Minutes' for full deploy, 'Seconds' for hot-swap, 'Minutes (includes upload overhead)' for CodeBuild. Alternatively, qualify with 'varies significantly based on stack complexity'.

issues.statement: Hot-swap is only available for updates to existing stacks. The first deployment of a new stage always runs a full deploy regardless of the flag. Not all Lambda functions in your stack are eligible for hot-swap. Stacktape determines eligibility automatically — if a function is ineligible, the deployment falls back to a full CloudFormation deploy.
issues.reason: The last sentence about individual function ineligibility causing a full CF deploy is misleading. Per the source, hot-swap eligibility is determined by the overall CloudFormation template diff (analyzeCloudformationTemplateDiff), not per-function. If the template diff is hot-swappable overall, individual functions are then checked for code changes. The fallback to full deploy happens when the template diff shows non-hot-swappable changes (e.g., IAM, new resources), not because a single function is 'ineligible'. The term 'allLambdasEligibleForHotswap' in config-manager refers to which Lambda types can participate in hot-swap (functions, deployment scripts, custom resources, SSR lambdas), not runtime eligibility.
issues.suggestedFix: Clarify that hot-swap availability is determined by the overall CloudFormation template diff. If any non-hot-swappable changes are detected (new resources, permission changes, etc.), the entire deployment falls back to full CloudFormation deploy. The 'eligible' distinction is about which Lambda types participate (user functions, SSR functions, deployment scripts) — not individual function runtime decisions.

positiveFindings: The deployment phase flow (validate → package → upload → deploy → post-deploy) is accurately grounded in the source code's event phases: BUILD_AND_PACKAGE, UPLOAD, DEPLOY, POST_DEPLOY.
The dev-mode guard is correctly documented — the source confirms `stpErrors.e141` is thrown when trying to deploy to an existing dev stack (line 46-51).
The auto-rollback artifact cleanup behavior is precisely documented, including the STACK_MONITORING exception where artifacts are preserved (source line 236).
The UPDATE_FAILED state cleanup logic is correctly described — source confirms `deleteArtifactsFixedDeploy()` runs after successful deploy over an UPDATE_FAILED stack (source line 243-245).
The CI/CD setup prompt on first deploy is correctly documented — source confirms `promptCiCdSetupAfterDeploy()` fires only when `stackActionType === 'create'` and invoked from CLI (line 199).
The `$Stage()` directive reference in the FAQ is confirmed — it exists as an exported function in `src/api/npm/ts/directives.ts` (line 102).

Hot-swap eligibility logic is mostly correct — the source confirms `analyzeCloudformationTemplateDiff` determines overall feasibility, and the fallback to full deploy with a warning message matches (source line 94).
The post-deploy steps (bucket sync, CDN invalidation, stack info save, notifications) are all correctly grounded in the source code (lines 146-185).
All cross-links point to valid routes in the documentation navigation index.
The obfuscated names warning accurately reflects the source message: 'Stack name too long (project+stage). Some resource names will be obfuscated.' (line 214).

Lambda hot-swap is correctly described as updating 'the deployed function code' only — the source confirms only Code and Tags property changes make a Lambda hot-swappable (hotswap-utils.ts line 48-49), which is more accurate than the curated docs that incorrectly claim Lambda env vars are hot-swappable.
verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded in the source code and accurately represents the Stacktape deployment pipeline. The deployment phases, auto-rollback behavior, hot-swap eligibility logic, post-deploy steps, and dev-mode guard are all correctly documented against the deploy command source. The Lambda hot-swap description is actually MORE accurate than the curated docs (correctly omitting env-var updates). Four issues identified: (1) the container hot-swap env-var claim is technically correct but needs a caveat about the ContainerDefinitions-only scope; (2) deployment duration estimates in the comparison table are ungrounded; (3) the per-function hot-swap eligibility description oversimplifies the template-diff-level decision; (4) the 'parallel packaging' claim is unverified from available source. All cross-links are valid, the $Stage() directive is confirmed, and no forbidden patterns (hand-authored YAML, StacktapeConfig type, fenced TS with defineConfig) are present.

The draft is mostly aligned with the deploy command source, but it overstates several details that are either only hinted at in old docs or not visible in the provided command source. The highest-impact fixes are to narrow post-deploy ordering, dev-mode rationale, stack-name shortening behavior, and unsupported timing/Console/CodeBuild details.

issues.statement: “After the stack is stable, Stacktape runs any user-defined `afterDeploy` hooks … Stacktape then syncs hosting buckets … invalidates CDN caches …”
issues.reason: The deploy command source shows bucket syncing and CDN invalidation inside `commandDeploy` before the completion comment that references afterDeploy hooks finishing. The source supports that afterDeploy hooks exist, but not the ordering claimed in the draft.
issues.suggestedFix: Avoid asserting that `afterDeploy` hooks run before bucket sync and CDN invalidation. Say that after a deploy, Stacktape may sync hosting buckets, invalidate CDN caches, save stack info, send notifications, and run configured `afterDeploy` hooks as part of deployment completion.

issues.statement: “Dev mode uses a lightweight infrastructure configuration optimized for local development, and deploying a full production config on top would create conflicts.”
issues.reason: The deploy command source supports that `stacktape deploy` rejects an existing dev stack, but it does not explain the implementation as a lightweight infrastructure configuration or state that a production config would create conflicts.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to the sourced behavior: “Stacktape blocks `stacktape deploy` against a stack created by dev mode. Use a different stage name for a deployed stack.”

issues.statement: “If the combined name is too long, Stacktape automatically shortens individual resource names to stay within AWS naming limits. You'll see a warning during deploy. This doesn't affect functionality, but resource names in the AWS console become less readable.”
issues.reason: The source only supports that a project+stage name can be too long and that some resource names are obfuscated. It does not support the specific mechanism “shortens individual resource names,” the purpose “to stay within AWS naming limits,” or the guarantee that functionality is unaffected.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the exact supported behavior: “If the project+stage stack name is too long, Stacktape warns that some resource names will be obfuscated. Keep project and stage names short when readable AWS resource names matter.”

issues.statement: The draft gives specific typical deployment durations: “Full deploy: 1-10 min,” “CodeBuild deploy: 3-15 min,” “A simple Lambda function deploys in under 2 minutes,” and “Stacks with databases, container workloads, and networking typically take 3–10 minutes.”
issues.reason: The provided sources do not establish these typical duration ranges. The curated doc gives one example output and a hot-swap estimate, but it does not support the broader timing ranges in the draft.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the unsourced numeric ranges or phrase duration qualitatively: full deployments depend on resource count and build size; hot-swap is intended for faster code iteration; CodeBuild adds startup/upload overhead but can help large container builds.

positiveFindings: The draft correctly describes the main deploy flow: initialize and validate, package workloads, resolve resources, prepare a CloudFormation template, upload artifacts, then perform hot-swap or full deploy.

The hot-swap fallback behavior is well grounded: the deploy source checks `globalStateManager.args.hotSwap`, analyzes the CloudFormation template diff, and warns before falling back to full CloudFormation deploy.
The dev-mode restriction’s core behavior is correctly captured: the deploy command checks stack metadata and rejects deploying over an existing dev-mode stack.
The rollback artifact cleanup caveat is accurately grounded: artifacts are cleaned up on failed full deploy when auto-rollback is enabled, except when the error type is `STACK_MONITORING`.
